nativeRange.habitat: Named after the Kedagang region of Borneo (Indonesian Kalimantan / Sarawak area), where wild Bucephalandra grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams; periodically emersed and resubmerged as water levels rise and fall

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line
do not bury the rhizome in substrate — leave it exposed to the water column
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; commonly used in foreground or midground placements
stable, established tank — generally advised against introducing into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra 'Kedagang' is a popular Borneo-endemic cultivar named for the Kedagang region; one of the larger and faster-growing Bucephalandra varieties, with dark green oblong leaves bearing white dots, a blue metallic sheen under water, dark red rhizomes, and copper-bronze leaf undersides.
careNotes: Bucephalandra 'Kedagang' is a named cultivar of the genus Bucephalandra (family Araceae), endemic to the island of Borneo and named after the Kedagang region. Husbandry is essentially identical to the parent genus entry (fw-plant-016): the creeping rhizome is attached to lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line, and is left exposed to the water column — burying the rhizome is generally reported to cause it to rot. Aquarium Co-Op describes the 'Kedagang' cultivar as having dark green, oblong leaves with wavy edges roughly 6 cm long by 1-2 cm wide, with small white dots, an iridescent blue sheen, and characteristic dark red rhizomes; KeepFishKeeping (KGTropicals) independently describes long, narrow dark green leaves with white dots up to about 6 cm long, red rhizomes, and a blue metallic shade when submerged. The cultivar is one of the larger Bucephalandras and is commonly reported to reach roughly 10-20 cm in height with good light and nutrition; Aquarium Co-Op describes it as one of the faster-growing buces, though growth is still slow by general aquarium-plant standards (commonly one new leaf every couple of weeks in low-tech setups). A distinctive feature noted by hobbyists is the copper-bronze coloration on the leaf undersides, which contrasts with the dark green upper surface and the blue structural iridescence. Lighting is flexible: low light supports survival growth, while medium light brings out the structural-color iridescence; CO2 is generally considered optional but tends to produce better growth and color. The plant absorbs nutrients from the water column, so substrate type is largely irrelevant and root tabs are not generally needed; light liquid fertilization is sufficient. 'Buce melt' (loss of original emersed-grown leaves after first submersion) is widely reported across the genus and should be expected with newly acquired tissue-cultured or potted Kedagang — as long as the rhizome stays firm, new submersed-form leaves are generally reported to emerge over the following weeks. Propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean scissors, ensuring each division has leaves, roots, and at least a few cm of rhizome, then glue the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division, the same as for the parent genus Bucephalandra. Once established, cut the rhizome at a natural bend or constriction with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe/spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Aquarium Co-Op describes the Kedagang cultivar as dark green oblong leaves with wavy edges ~6 cm long by 1-2 cm wide, small white dots, iridescent blue sheen, dark red rhizomes, mature height ~10-20 cm, and one of the faster-growing buce cultivars. Offered as tissue culture.

additional.name: KeepFishKeeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked Borneo origin, dark green narrow leaves with white dots up to ~6 cm long, red rhizomes, and blue metallic submerged coloration. Page does not give explicit water parameters; husbandry parameters inherited from the parent genus entry (fw-plant-016).

additional.name: AquaMate parent entry: Bucephalandra (fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Genus-level husbandry (water parameters 22-28 °C / pH 6-7.5 / gH 3-15, low-to-medium light, optional CO2, rhizome-attachment to hardscape, propagation by rhizome division, 'buce melt' acclimation) used as the canonical care reference for this cultivar; care for named cultivars including 'Kedagang' is reported across hobby sources to be essentially identical to the parent genus.
